title: Pet Medication Dosage Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Pet Medication Dosage by Weight
description: Calculate exact dog and cat medication doses in mg and ml based on pet weight, drug concentration, and frequency. Verify vet prescriptions instantly.
seoKeywords: how much medication for my dog
intro: **IMPORTANT—READ FIRST**: This calculator **does NOT replace a veterinarian**. It's a reference tool to **verify the dosage your vet already prescribed**—useful if you're unsure about the ml or mg amount, your pet's weight has changed, or you need a refresher on administration instructions. Enter your **pet's weight (kg)**, the **prescribed dose (mg/kg)** from the prescription or product insert, the **drug concentration (mg/ml)** shown on the label, and **doses per day**. It calculates **total daily dose in mg**, **total daily volume in ml**, and **amount per dose in ml**. Never give human medications to your dog or cat without explicit veterinary approval—common drugs like acetaminophen and ibuprofen are **lethal** to pets. If you don't have a prescription, don't use this calculator to guess—call your veterinarian or visit an emergency vet clinic.
keyTakeaway: **NEVER medicate without veterinary guidance.** Formula: `total_dose_mg = pet_weight_kg × dose_mg/kg`. Volume: `ml_total = total_mg / concentration_mg/ml`. Per dose: `ml_total / doses_per_day`. **Acetaminophen and ibuprofen are LETHAL to cats and dogs.**
useCases: Your vet prescribed an antibiotic and you want to double-check the exact ml amount before giving it.
Your pet's weight has changed and you need to adjust a chronic medication dose.
You have the product insert with mg/kg and the bottle concentration, but aren't sure how many ml to use.
You're giving meloxicam or carprofen (NSAIDs) and want to verify the dosage one more time.
Your pet's weight is atypical (e.g., a 2 kg kitten, a 60 kg giant breed dog) and the label doesn't cover it.

fields.label: Pet weight (kg)
fields.help: Your pet's weight in kilograms. Weigh on a scale (most vets have one).

fields.label: Prescribed dose (mg/kg)
fields.help: Found on your vet's prescription or the medication insert

fields.label: Drug concentration (mg/ml)
fields.help: e.g., amoxicillin suspension 50 mg/ml. Check the medication label.

fields.label: Doses per day
fields.help: How many times per day (e.g., 2 for every 12 hours)

dataUpdate.notes: Pure pharmacological formula: mg/kg per concentration.

example.title: Real example: 15 kg dog prescribed amoxicillin by vet
steps: **Dose per administration (mg)**: `15 kg × 10 mg/kg` = **150 mg per dose**.
**Volume per dose (ml)**: `150 mg / 50 mg/ml` = **3 ml per dose**.
example.result: Give your dog **3 ml of suspension every 12 hours** (morning and evening). Total daily: 6 ml. Typical antibiotic course: **7–10 days complete**, even if your dog seems better sooner—stopping early allows infection to return stronger and resistant.

explanation:
## ⚠️ Important Warning

**This calculator is a reference tool only and does NOT replace veterinary consultation.** Before giving any medication:

1. **Consult your veterinarian** for the correct prescription.
2. **Verify the dose in mg/kg** on the prescription or product insert.
3. **Check the concentration** (mg/ml) on the medication label.
4. **NEVER give human medication** without explicit veterinary approval.
5. If your pet shows severe symptoms, **go to an emergency vet clinic immediately**—don't delay calculating a dose.

## Calculation Formula

```
Dose per administration (mg) = weight_kg × dose_mg/kg
Total daily dose (mg)         = dose_per_admin × doses_per_day
Volume per administration (ml) = dose_per_admin / concentration_mg/ml
Total daily volume (ml)        = volume_per_admin × doses_per_day
```

**Complete example**: **15 kg dog** prescribed **10 mg/kg amoxicillin every 12 hours**. Oral suspension **50 mg/ml**:

```
Per dose    = 15 × 10 = 150 mg per dose
Total/day   = 150 × 2 = 300 mg/day
Volume      = 150 / 50 = 3 ml per dose
Total ml/day = 3 × 2 = 6 ml
```

## Common Veterinary Medications (Reference)

> **Always with veterinary prescription.** These are reference dosage ranges.

### Antibiotics

| Medication | Typical Dose | Frequency | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amoxicillin** | 10–20 mg/kg | every 12 hrs | General bacterial infection |
| **Amoxicillin + clavulanic acid** | 12.5–25 mg/kg | every 12 hrs | Complex infections |
| **Enrofloxacin** (Baytril) | 5–20 mg/kg | every 24 hrs | Gram-negative, urinary |
| **Metronidazole** | 15 mg/kg | every 12 hrs | Diarrhea, giardia, anaerobes |
| **Cephalexin** | 15–30 mg/kg | every 12 hrs | Skin, urinary |
| **Doxycycline** | 5–10 mg/kg | every 24 hrs | Rickettsial, respiratory |

### Anti-Inflammatories (NSAIDs) — Pain & Inflammation

| Medication | Dog Dose | Cat Dose | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Meloxicam** (Metacam) | 0.1 mg/kg (day 1: 0.2) | 0.05 mg/kg | every 24 hrs |
| **Carprofen** (Rimadyl) | 2.2 mg/kg | NOT APPROVED | every 12 hrs |
| **Firocoxib** (Previcox) | 5 mg/kg | NOT APPROVED | every 24 hrs |
| **Robenacoxib** (Onsior) | 1–2 mg/kg | 1 mg/kg | every 24 hrs, up to 6 days |

### Corticosteroids

| Medication | Anti-Inflammatory Dose | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Prednisone/Prednisolone** | 0.5–1 mg/kg every 24 hrs | Allergies, inflammation |
| **Dexamethasone** | 0.1–0.2 mg/kg | Short-term only |

### Stomach Protectants

| Medication | Dose | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Omeprazole** | 0.5–1 mg/kg every 24 hrs | Ulcers, reflux |
| **Famotidine** | 0.5–1 mg/kg every 12 hrs | Gastritis |
| **Sucralfate** | 0.5–1 g every 8 hrs (dogs) | Ulcers |

### Antiparasitic Medications

| Medication | Dose | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Ivermectin** | 0.2 mg/kg (ONE-TIME DOSE) | ⚠️ AVOID in Collies, Australian Shepherds |
| **Praziquantel** | 5 mg/kg | Tapeworms |
| **Pyrantel** | 5 mg/kg | Internal parasites |
| **Fluralaner** (Bravecto) | 25 mg/kg | Fleas & ticks 3 months |

## ⚠️ Human Medications Toxic to Pets

These common human drugs are dangerous or lethal even in small doses:

| Medication | Toxicity | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Acetaminophen (Tylenol)** | 🚨 LETHAL in cats (even small dose) | Cats lack proper liver enzymes |
| **Ibuprofen** | 🚨 TOXIC in dogs & cats | GI ulcers, kidney failure |
| **Aspirin** | TOXIC (except tiny doses in dogs) | Bleeding, ulcers |
| **Naproxen** | 🚨 HIGHLY TOXIC | Narrow safety window |
| **Diclofenac** | 🚨 TOXIC | Kidney failure |
| **Xylitol** (sweetener, gum, toothpaste) | 🚨 LETHAL | Severe hypoglycemia |
| **Chocolate** (theobromine) | TOXIC | Dark chocolate worse |
| **Grapes/raisins** | 🚨 TOXIC (dogs) | Acute kidney failure |
| **Onions/garlic** | TOXIC | Destroys red blood cells |
| **Avocado** (pit/skin) | TOXIC | Persin toxin |
| **Macadamia nuts** | TOXIC (dogs) | Neurological |
| **Caffeine** (coffee, tea, energy drinks) | TOXIC | Nervous system |
| **Alcohol** | 🚨 LETHAL | Different metabolism |

## How to Administer Correctly

### Liquid Oral Medications (Syrup/Suspension)

1. **Shake the bottle well** before measuring.
2. **Draw exact dose into syringe** (no needle).
3. **Insert syringe at the side of the mouth** (between molars and cheek, not down the center of tongue).
4. Inject slowly so your pet can swallow—don't force it.
5. If your pet coughs or chokes, wait and retry with less at a time.

### Tablets / Pills

- **Hide in sticky food**: cream cheese, peanut butter, liverwurst, wet bread, yogurt.
- **Pill pockets** (premade treats): convenient but pricey.
- **Pill crusher** (for dogs that won't swallow): only if vet approves (some meds lose effectiveness when crushed).
- **Hide tablet in butter or coconut oil**: sometimes works for reluctant pets.

### Subcutaneous Injections

Some chronic treatments (insulin, certain antibiotics) require home injections. If your vet trained you:
- Use the neck area (between shoulder blades)
- New syringe every time
- 45-degree angle
- Follow vet's training exactly

## Golden Rules for Medicating Pets

1. **Complete the entire course**, even if your pet seems better by day 3. Especially antibiotics.
2. **Write down the time** you give each dose (phone note, calendar, app like PetDesk).
3. **Same time every day**—if "every 12 hours" starts at 8 am, give it 8 am and 8 pm consistently.
4. **With or without food depends on the drug**—some irritate an empty stomach, others absorb better with fat. Ask your vet.
5. **Never eyeball doses** from human tablets—ask your vet for a veterinary formulation or prepare an exact dilution.
6. **Store medications securely** out of your pet's reach—don't trust high shelves.
7. **Check expiration dates**, especially liquids.

## Signs of Overdose or Toxicity

🚨 **Emergency—go to vet or call pet poison control if you see:**

- Persistent vomiting.
- Bloody diarrhea.
- Extreme lethargy, won't respond.
- Tremors or seizures.
- Loss of coordination, stumbling.
- Excessive panting or rapid breathing.
- Pale, blue, or white gums.
- Pinpoint or very dilated pupils.
- Excessive urination or inability to urinate.

## What to Do in Case of Poisoning

1. **Call your vet immediately** or go to a 24-hour emergency clinic.
2. **Bring the medication bottle or packaging**.
3. **Note the time of exposure** and approximate amount ingested.
4. **Do NOT induce vomiting** without vet approval—some substances cause more harm coming back up.
5. **ASPCA Animal Poison Control**: (888) 426-4435 (open 24/7, small fee applies).
6. **Pet Poison Helpline**: (855) 764-7661 (24/7, paid service).
7. **Have local 24-hour emergency vet numbers** saved in your phone.

## Related Calculators

- [Dog Age Calculator](/calculadora-edad-perro-anos-humanos) – adjust care based on life stage.
- [Cat Age Calculator](/calculadora-edad-gato-anos-humanos) – know when your cat is a senior.
- [Daily Dog Food](/calculadora-alimento-diario-perro) – correct calories by weight.
- [Pet Ideal Weight](/calculadora-alimento-diario-perro) – monitor for obesity.

faq.q: Can I give my dog or cat ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain?
faq.a: **NO. NEVER.** Acetaminophen is **LETHAL to cats** even in one dose. It's also highly toxic to dogs. Ibuprofen causes severe stomach ulcers and kidney failure in both species. If your pet is in pain, take them to a vet—safe NSAIDs like **meloxicam** (Metacam) and **carprofen** (Rimadyl, dogs only) exist specifically for this. Never improvise with human over-the-counter pain relievers.

faq.q: Do cats need different medication doses than dogs?
faq.a: **YES.** Cats metabolize drugs very differently—they lack certain liver enzymes (glucuronyl transferase) that dogs have. Many dog medications are **toxic to cats at equivalent doses**. Examples: carprofen, aspirin, acetaminophen, permetrin (flea treatment). Even when the same drug is safe for both, the dose often differs: meloxicam for dogs is 0.1 mg/kg, but cats get **0.05 mg/kg** (half). Always ask for a cat-specific prescription—never extrapolate from a dog's dose.

faq.q: What if I miss a dose?
faq.a: **If you remember within 4 hours** of the scheduled time, give it immediately and resume the normal schedule. **If more than 4 hours has passed**, skip that dose and give the next one on time. **Never double up** on a missed dose—this causes overdose, especially with NSAIDs (ulcers) or heart medications. Use phone alarms or a medication app like **PetDesk** to avoid forgetting.

faq.q: How do I verify my vet's dosage is correct?
faq.a: Use the formula: `dose = weight_kg × mg/kg`. If your vet wrote "150 mg every 12 hours for a 15 kg dog," that's 150 ÷ 15 = **10 mg/kg**—reasonable for amoxicillin (10–20 mg/kg range). If the math looks way off (e.g., 500 mg for a 3 kg cat = 166 mg/kg—far too high), **call your vet before giving it**. Some drugs like Baytril are dosed as a **total daily amount, not per dose**—read the insert carefully.

faq.q: What if my pet vomits shortly after taking medication?
faq.a: **If vomiting occurs within 30 minutes** and you see the pill in the vomit, **contact your vet**—the dose usually needs to be repeated. **If it's been more than 2 hours**, the medication likely absorbed, so **don't repeat the dose**. **If vomiting is frequent** (2+ times), call your vet urgently—it may be an adverse reaction and you may need to stop the medication. Keep a record of the exact time and whether the pill/liquid was visible.

faq.q: Can I split a pill to give a smaller dose?
faq.a: **It depends.** Pills with a **center line/groove are designed to split**. **Never split:** capsules (contents spill), enteric-coated tablets (acid-resistant coating breaks), extended-release pills (dumps full dose at once). Ask your vet or pharmacist. For small pets (kittens, tiny dogs), liquid suspensions are ideal—they allow exact ml dosing. Many vets can dispense crushed or liquid formulations if splitting isn't an option.

faq.q: How long does liquid pet medication last once opened?
faq.a: **Usually 14 days refrigerated** (2–8°C), but check the label. Amoxicillin suspension: 14 days. Clavamox: 10 days. Metronidazole: 30 days. **Always read the package insert**—expiration varies by drug. If the liquid changes color, separates oddly, smells off, or past its printed discard date, **don't use it**. Store in the refrigerator in a dark place.

faq.q: Can I stop antibiotics early if my pet seems better?
faq.a: **No. Finish the entire course.** Your pet feels better by day 2–3 because weak bacteria die first. If you stop early, **stronger, antibiotic-resistant bacteria survive and return**—this is a major public health problem. The minimum course is **7 days** for most infections; **10–14+ days** for urinary, skin, and chronic infections. Finish every dose even if your pet looks perfect. Mark a calendar or phone reminder so you don't forget.

faq.q: Which human medications are most dangerous for pets?
faq.a: The deadliest: **acetaminophen (lethal in cats), ibuprofen, naproxen, xylitol (artificial sweetener), grapes/raisins, chocolate, onions, garlic, macadamia nuts, and alcohol**. NSAIDs cause kidney failure and stomach ulcers. Xylitol (found in gum, sugar-free treats, some toothpastes) causes sudden hypoglycemia—even a few grams can be fatal. **Never assume 'it won't hurt'**—when in doubt, call poison control: **ASPCA (888) 426-4435**.

faq.q: How do I measure liquid medication accurately for my cat?
faq.a: Use a **syringe without the needle**—most vets provide them free with liquid prescriptions. Draw the exact ml amount into the syringe. Insert at the **side of the mouth** (between the molars and cheek), **not down the center of the tongue**. Inject slowly so your cat can swallow. Never squirt fast—your cat may aspirate. Mark your syringe or bottle with a permanent marker at the fill line so you don't accidentally overdose. Store syringes clean and dry between doses.
